logInterpretation: Chronological order: connecting (14s) -> WebSocket 'error' event (18s) -> 'close' event (18s) -> reconnect after backoff (23s, 5s = max BACKOFF_STEPS so the loop was already advanced). Both the error and close handlers ran for a single socket. The state never became 'tokenError' or 'protocolError'.

closesWith1008PolicyViolationWhen: extension.hello token != ConnectionToken (sends protocol.error invalid_token, then CloseAsync PolicyViolation)
extension.hello protocolVersion != chrome-lab-v1 (sends protocol.error protocol_version_mismatch, then CloseAsync PolicyViolation)

conclusion: Because M637D showed neither 'tokenError' nor 'protocolError' and no protocol.error was surfaced, the socket close did NOT originate from the bridge auth/protocol path. A bridge-initiated close would have produced 'tokenError'/'protocolError' and stopped reconnecting. The observed generic 'error'/'reconnecting' close (inferred 1006) is consistent with either (a) transport-level failure (bridge process not reachable at the moment of connect) or (b) client-side socket churn from competing sockets. The competing-socket race is the demonstrable, fixable client-side cause and is addressed in M637E.
closeReasonObservedOrInferred: no bridge protocol.error => not invalid_token / not protocol mismatch
remainingManualEvidenceNeededForFullCert: DevTools Network panel: the actual WebSocket close code (1006 vs 1008) and whether the handshake reached HTTP 101
Confirmation the bridge process was listening on 127.0.0.1:8787 during the test
DevTools Console: any CSP violation or ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED lines
